coredata.dc:title: Enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity of ZnS/S-graphene quantum dots reinforced with Ag2S nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Nekooei, Aida
Miroliaei, Mohammad Reza
Shahabi Nejad, Mohaddeseh
Sheibani, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: Today, contamination of water resources by pesticides and their presence in the food chain is one of the environmental problems. An economical method to eliminate of pesticides is the degradation in the presence of sunlight, which intensifies the need to design and manufacture new generation photocatalysts with short gap bands to accelerate the process of optical degradation. In this study, sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (SG QDs) were successfully synthesized by the hydrothermal method and stabilized on the surface of ZnS semiconductor nanoparticles. Then, the core–shell structure of ZnS-SG QDs was decorated and reinforced with Ag2S nanoparticles. After confirming the structure of the prepared photocatalyst by X-ray diffraction (XRD), fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), scanning, and transmission electron microscopy (SEM & TEM) analyses, the photocatalyst's ability to optically degrade the two organophosphorus toxins diazinon and fenitrothion was evaluated. The results showed that the presence of Ag2S improved the photocatalytic activity and increased the degradation efficiencies to amount 40% for diazinon and fenitrothion. Furthermore, kinetic study showed that the photocatalytic degradation is initially followed by first order model and after that the mechanism is possibly changed to the second order model.

head.ce:title: Enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity of ZnS/S-graphene quantum dots reinforced with Ag2S nanoparticles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Today, contamination of water resources by pesticides and their presence in the food chain is one of the environmental problems. An economical method to eliminate of pesticides is the degradation in the presence of sunlight, which intensifies the need to design and manufacture new generation photocatalysts with short gap bands to accelerate the process of optical degradation. In this study, sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (SG QDs) were successfully synthesized by the hydrothermal method and stabilized on the surface of ZnS semiconductor nanoparticles. Then, the core–shell structure of ZnS-SG QDs was decorated and reinforced with Ag2S nanoparticles. After confirming the structure of the prepared photocatalyst by X-ray diffraction (XRD), fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), scanning, and transmission electron microscopy (SEM & TEM) analyses, the photocatalyst's ability to optically degrade the two organophosphorus toxins diazinon and fenitrothion was evaluated. The results showed that the presence of Ag2S improved the photocatalytic activity and increased the degradation efficiencies to amount 40% for diazinon and fenitrothion. Furthermore, kinetic study showed that the photocatalytic degradation is initially followed by first order model and after that the mechanism is possibly changed to the second order model.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the growth of industries, population growth, depletion of water resources and the indiscriminate use of hazardous chemicals in various sectors, as well as the strengthening of environmental protection laws, it has caused studies to be pursued more intensively to provide effective elimination systems. Given the development of agriculture and the diversity of pests, the increasing use of pesticides around the world, can be a threat to human health and natural ecosystems. Organophosphorus compounds are the most widely used insecticides in the world [1–3]. Reports indicate that large parts of the earth's water and ecosystems are contaminated with these compounds [4,5]. Nowadays, the variety pesticides with systemic characteristics such as digestive, penetrating, fumigating are widely used due to rapid action and rapid metabolism in living organisms [6–9]. Diazinon and fenitrothion are organic phosphorus pesticides which widely used in developing countries [10], the solubility of these toxins in water is 40 and 38 mg/L at 25 °C, respectively [11,12]. These values indicate that mentioned toxins are highly mobile in the soil and although they are rarely absorbed through the skin, they are readily absorbed through the lungs and spread throughout the body without accumulation in the tissues [13–15]. Since, diazinon and fenitrothion that can dissolve in fat and oils, so they can be toxicity if the significant amounts of pesticides are stored in adipose tissue [16–18]. The most common methods used to remove pesticides are: thermal adsorption [19,20], washing of contaminated soils [21–23], biodegradation [24–27], soil treatment [28], chemical processes and direct oxidation processes [29–31]. Due to the microbial resistance of organophosphorus pesticides, bioremoval methods do not have the desired results and instead advanced oxidation processes have been considered [32]. Optical decomposition is one of the natural and economic mechanisms for the treatment of xenobiotic substances in wastewater [33–35]. In recent decades, the serious and growing energy crisis has led to extensive research into the conversion of optical energy into chemical energy by photocatalytic processes which the semiconductor nanoparticles are used in this kind of processes [36,37]. In semiconductor materials, energy levels are not continuous, and there is an energy gap between the valance band and the conduction band which by emitting photons with the appropriate wavelength, the electron is excited and transferred from the capacitance band to the conduction band. As a result, an extra electron is produced in the conduction band along with producing an electron void (hole) in the valance band [38–39]. The excited electron can produce hydroxyl radicals in the presence of oxygen and aqueous solution, which is very active and destroys pollutants, eventually leading to the production of CO2 and H2O [40].

Among the used semiconductors in advanced oxidation reactions, the compounds such as titanium dioxide, indium oxide, manganese dioxide, zinc oxide and tungsten trioxide, and sulfides such as zinc sulfide and cadmium sulfide mostly used [41–45]. ZnS is a non-toxic metal sulfide semiconductor of category IV-II and one of the first semiconductors to be discovered usually in two structural phases, the long structure of zinc (Sphalerite) with cubic and hexagonal phases and the Wurtzite structure are found. The band gap energy of the two ZnS phases (cubic and hexagonal phases) is 3.7–5.5 eV and 3.8–7.7 eV, respectively [46]. Zinc sulfide is also an important substance with different luminescence properties such as electroluminescence (EL) and photoluminescence (PL) which used in sensors, lasers and monitors. The use of sulfide has advantages such as the rapid photon excitation and formation of electron-hole pairs, more negative reduction potential than relative to standard hydrogen electrode (SHE), and antibacterial properties [47]. However, due to the large energy gap, its excitation is limited to UV light, which contains only 5% of the sunlight that reaches the earth, and in addition, the recombination of the electron-hole pair reduces its efficiency [48]. To solve these problems, much researchs and studies have been done to use visible light to stimulate photocatalysts and to prevent recombination of charge carriers. These methods include metal doping, coupling with semiconductors with lower energy gaps such as CdS, CuS, Ag2S and sensitize of the semiconductor to visible light using dyes, polymers, complexes and especially graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [49–51].

GQDs are the product of the chemical oxidation process of carbonaceous materials [52]. They can be considered an extremely small derivative of graphene oxide, contains a significant number of oxygenated functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl which makes it possible to connect them to semiconductor materials. [53–54] In addition, GQDs have large surface area, biocompatibility, low toxicity, good solubility in water, stable photoluminescence, adjustable energy gap and ability to absorb visible light [55]. These extraordinary physical and chemical properties make graphene quantum dots suitable for use as sensors, DNA splitters, light emitting diodes, biological images, photovoltaics, markers, catalysts and photocatalysts [56–58]. However, the existence of some disadvantages such as aggregation and tendency to mass and subsequent reduction of surface properties prevents the use of these materials as a single component [59] that to solve this problem, GQDs are immobilized on other nanomaterial surface to form core–shell or composites.

Another method to improve the optical properties of semiconductors and reduce the electron-hole recombination rate is doping with non-metallic and metal atoms [60,61]. The most famous non-metallic dopants are nitrogen and sulfur, which increase the rate of radical hydroxyl formation and of course, the rate of optical degradation by increasing the absorption of dissolved oxygen on the surface of semiconductors [62,63]. Metal dopant contains noble metals such as Pd, Pt, Au and Ag [64–66]. Among noble metals, silver is a very good choice due to its remarkable catalytic potential, non-toxicity and cost-effectiveness, as well as antibacterial properties compared to other noble metals [67]. This metal dopant to form of Ag2S, not only does it enhance the antibacterial properties but it also absorbs a wide range of sunlight. Silver sulfides can be easily reduced to metallic Ag under photoreduction [68]. The metallic Ag in role of electron capturer can facilitate interfacial charge transfer and with a bandwidth of 0.9–1.05 eV can have a big impact on the bandgap narrowing of the final photocatalyst

Band gap narrowing can be done either by shifting the conduction band minimum in the downward direction or by elevating the valence band maximum in the upward direction. However, shifting of band edge to a large extent will influence the photocatalyst material's oxidizing and reducing properties. Therefore, one needs to employ a controlled bandgap engineering. In this study, we changed and tuned the bandgap of ZnS to enhance its visible light absorption behavior. A ternary based heterostructure of S-GQDs, Ag2S and ZnS with enhanced photocatalytic activity is a promising material for visible-light-driven photocatalyst. The main objective of this study is to show whether the ternary heterostructure based on S-GQDs and Ag2S will enhance the electronic properties and the visible light response of ZnS. For this purpose, Ag2S nanoparticles were stabilized on the core–shell structure of ZnS/S-GQDs, and its photocatalytic ability was investigated in the decomposition of diazinon and fenitrothion under sunlight.

Thiourea (99%), Silver nitrate (99%) and Citric acid (99.5%) were purchased from Merck- Millipore chemical Co. and used as received. Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (98%), and Sodium sulfide nonahydrate (99%)were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. The diazinon and fenitrothion with purities 35% and 50% respectively were obtained from (Arya chemistry Company, Iran). The prepared ZnS/SG QDs/Ag2S photocatalyst was characterized by fourier transform infrared (FT‐IR, Bruker, Germany, RT-DLATGS detector) spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, TESCAN-MIRA3), transmission electron microscope (TEM, EM10c-100 KV) and the concentration of pesticides solutions have been monitored using Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy was performed in the range of 200–900 nm wavelength on a Cary 50 single detector double beam in time spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia).

The synthesis of ZnS quantum dots was carried out at room temperature without using any organic solvent by chemical co-precipitation method [69]. For this purpose, 100 mL Zinc nitrate hexahydrate solution (0.1 mol/L) was poured into 2-neck Round bottom flask and then the thiourea as an inhibitor agent with equal concentration was poured into the dropping funnel and was added dropwise into the flask. The reaction mixture was stirred under nitrogen gas (to remove dissolved oxygen) and then, 100 mL sodium sulfide solution (0.1 mol/L) was added dropwise to the flask. After the completion reaction, the synthesized ZnS quantum dots was centrifuged. The obtained precipitated was washed several times with deionized water and dried at 50 °C.

There are two general methods for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots, which involve first method: Top-down such as discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical synthesis, nanometer etching, and special oxidation cleavage, second method: bottom-up includes combustion, pyrolyzation, microwave assistant pyrolysis, solvothermal, and, hydrothermal. In this research, S-graphene QDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method [70]. Briefly, a mixture of citric acid (1.5 g), thiourea (1.5 g) and distilled water (50 mL) was stirred for 15 min until formation of transparent solution. Then the reaction mixture was transferred to the teflon autoclave and placed at 180 °C for 4 h. Consequently, the color of the mixture turned to glossy yellow. Finally, the obtained S-graphene QDs were dialyzed to remove large particles for 12 h.

To cover of the ZnS QDs by S-G QDs, first 500 mg ZnS QDs and 100 mL deionized water were exposed to ultrasound waves for 15 min, then 10 mL of previous S-G QDs were added to the above suspension and the reaction mixture was stirred at ambient temperature for 24 h. The resulting suspension was centrifuged and washed several times with deionized water to remove the impurities, and dried under vacuum.

In the following, to synthesize Ag2S nanoparticles from silver nitrate source on the QDs support, the prepared ZnS /S-G QDs (500 mg) were suspended in 200 mL of deionized water by sonication. Soon afterward, 50 mg of silver nitrate powder together 70 mg sodium sulfide nonahydrate were dissolved in the above solution and kept stirring for 5 h at room temperature. The ZnS/S-G QDs/Ag2S photocatalyst was separated from the reaction mixture by centrifugation, washed with deionized water three times and dried under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the photocatalytic activity of ZnS /S-G QDs/Ag2S
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to investigate the photocatalytic properties of the prepared ZnS/S-G QDs/Ag2S, some experiments were performed on a catalytic photoreactor with a visible light source (LED light bulbs −60 W). In a typical experiment, 5 mg of prepared photocatalyst and 25 mL of contaminated solution (10 mg/L) (diazinon and fenitrothion) after suspension by ultrasound, was placed in a dark place for 30 min to establish adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Then, the reaction mixture was irradiated visible light for different periods of time and for sampling, 5 mL of suspension was taken every 10 min by a syringe filter (Millex-GP, 0.22 µm) to remove solid particles before analysis. In the end, to determine the efficiency of pollutant degradation, the adsorption of diazinon and fenitrothion by UV– Vis-spectrophotometer at 247 and 269 nm (maximum wavenumber) was read and the percentage of degradation was calculated according to Eq. (1).
(1)
Photocatalytic destruction
%
=
C 0
- C
/
C 0
×
100

In this regard, C 0 is the initial concentration of the respective contaminant and C is the concentration of the remaining contaminant in the solution, after the photodegradation reaction. It should be noted that all experiments were repeated twice and the average value was reported. In addition, the COD of the samples was measured every hour by FAS titration with potassium permanganate (Method No. 5220D) [71].

The kinetic of photocatalytic degradation of pesticides in heterogeneous oxidation systems with visible light follows the Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model [72] (Eq. (2)).
(2)

where r, C, t, k and K are the oxidation rate (mg/L min), pesticide concentration (mg/L), irradiation time (min), reaction rate constant (1/min) and reaction adsorption coefficient (L/mg), respectively. At low initial concentrations of pesticides, Equation (2) is changed to the quasi-first-order equation (Eq. (3)).

By plotting ln (C/C0) versus t, the slope of the diagram shows the apparent rate of photocatalytic degradation.

Quantum dots are zero-dimensional semiconductor nanocrystals with a physical diameter less than the exciton Bohr radius [73]. The importance of semiconductor quantum dots is that the electrical conductivity of these materials can be altered by external stimuli such as an electric field or light radiation, to the point that they become conductive and act as a switch [74]. For this reason, it is widely used in the manufacture of light-emitting diodes, lasers, medical imaging, printer inks, sensors, and photocatalysts. In the present project, ZnS QDs were used to make a photocatalyst, because these have lower toxicity and greater environmental compatibility compared to other metal quantum dots [75].

Of course, the bulk ZnS has a relatively high energy gap, which becomes even larger when it becomes quantum dots [76]. Also, these zero-dimensional particles tend to agglomeration which to address these deficiencies, ZnS QDs were coated immediately after synthesis with additional operations [77]. The coating of ZnS QDs are usually done with a layer of another semiconductor with a suitable energy gap to improve the photoluminescence properties and also to prevent the release of metal ions from the core to the surrounding environment. S-graphene QDs are the suitable candidate for this purpose due to the presence of sulfur atoms along with increasing flexibility and also the tendency to attaching onto ZnS QDs [78]. The importance of using SG can be justified from another point of view. The presence of heteroatoms such as sulfur on the surface of the photocatalyst support increases the absorption of dissolved oxygen and leads accelerates the formation of hydroxyl radicals and of course, optical degradation [79]. After creating the core–shell structure of ZnS/SG QDs, another problem arises, and that is the high rate of electron-hole recombination, which reduces photocatalytic degradation. To modify the recombination rate, the use of noble metals nanoparticles such as silver is recommended, so in the last stage of photocatalyst fabrication, silver sulfide nanoparticles were loaded which can be easily reduced to metallic Ag under photoreduction. The bandgaps of ZnS QDs, SG QDs and Ag2S NPs are around 3.5–4.0 eV, 2.2–3.1 eV and 0.9–1.1 eV respectively. Utilizing these compounds together leads to the optimization of the final composite bandgap which has the highest photocatalytic activity under visible light (Scheme 1 ).

Fig. 1 shows the FTIR spectra of ZnS QDs (Fig. 1a), ZnS/SG QDs (Fig. 1b) and ZnS/SG QDs/Ag2S (Fig. 1c). In the ZnSQDs spectrum, vibration peaks around 560 and 670 cm−1 are assigned to symmetric and asymmetric bending vibrations of Zn-S bonds respectively. Also, two appeared peaks at 1621 and 3412 cm−1 corresponding to the bending and stretching vibration of adsorbed water hydroxyl [80].

After coating the surface of ZnS QDs with SG QDs, the numbers of new couriers appeared in the Fig. 1b for example, the adsorption band at 1670 can be attributed to the CC, CO vibrations which overlap and are the main bands of quantum dots. Furthermore, the CO symmetric stretching vibration of carboxylate (1320 cm−1) and C-S stretching vibration (498 cm−1) along with H-C-S bending vibration (819 cm−1) confirm the formation of ZnS/SG QDs.

In the final step of photocatalyst synthesis, with immobilizing of Ag2S nanoparticles on the surface was not occurred principle alteration in the spectrum. The only, Flattening was observed in the absorption bands, which could be due to the interaction of Ag2S nanoparticles with ZnS/SG QDs functional groups.

X-ray diffraction analysis is used to investigate the crystalline nature and particle size Which the XRD patterns are shown in Fig. 2 . In order to investigate the formation of photocatalysts, their XRD patterns were compared with Ag2S and ZnS standard cards. As in Fig. 2a is visible, the three main peaks of this spectrum at angles 2θ = 30, 48, and 57 correspond to Miller indexs (311) (220) and (111), respectively, corresponding to the cubic structure of zinc sulfide (JCPDS NO. 80–20) [81]. In the catalyst diffraction pattern, compared with the XRD peaks of the ZnS NPs, the three main diffraction peaks of ZnS /S-GQDs Ag2S NPs are broadened. This is maybe induced by the amorphous structure of S-GQDs. The ZnS NPs peak appearing at ∼57°, which corresponds to the (311) planes, is significantly suppressed. This suggests that ZnS is covered by something, which accounted for the suppression of (311) plane. Also there are new peaks that all new diffraction peaks around 2D = 20–65 can be indexed to the pure monoclinic Ag2S (PDF 00-024-0715) [82–83] and the appearance of sharp lateral peaks at 2D = 19, possibly associated with periodic changes in network distance or the presence of SG-QDs (Fig. 2b).

The size of the crystals can also be estimated using the X-ray diffraction pattern. To calculate the crystal size, a method should be used that considered the effect of stress and consequently the network strain in calculating the crystal size. For this reason, were used the Williamson-Hall method (Eq. (4)) [84].
(4)
β
cos θ =
0.89 λ / d +
4 ε sin θ

In this equation, λ is the X-ray wavelength (usually a copper lamp in which λ is 1.5406 Å), D is the mean size of nanocrystals, A is constant (usually A = 1), ε is the mean strain, β is the peak width at half height (in radians) and θ is the Bragg angle. By plotting the data related to βCos θ versus 4Sin θ for all peaks in the X-ray diffraction pattern, a straight line is obtained who’s its slope is the strain and the width from the origin is the size of crystals. According to this, the crystal size of ZnS QDs and Ag2S were 3.9 and 28 nm.

It mattersthat the main characteristic of semiconductors is the width of their energy gap. One of the ways to calculate is the use of the well-known Tuac equation [85] (5), which is written as follows.
(5)
( α ⊖ ν )
( 1 / n )
= A
( ⊖ ν - Eg )

Here, A is constant, α is the absorption coefficient, Eg is the width of the energy gap, and hν is the energy of the emitting photon with respect to the absorption spectrum of the synthesized nanoparticles. Also, n for zinc sulfide with a straight and permissible bandgap is equal to 1/2. According to Fig. 3 , the energy gap of zinc sulfide QDs can be calculated by drawing (αhν)2(eV·cm)2 in versus of hν (eV) and extrapolation its linear part. This energy gap was obtained 3.94 eV. Note that the energy gap of the prepared ZnS QDs is higher than normal (3.6 eV), due to the effects of quantum constraint.

SEM analysis was used to investigate the morphology of the sample. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the sample is composed of very fine particles that adhere to each other, creating a uniform surface qua it is not easy to determine the boundary between particles due to their very small size and the large number of them. The reason for the adhesion of the particles is due to their very high surface energy. In fact, due to the very small size of the obtained particles, their surface area is very high and they stick together to minimize the surface energy as much as possible.

Also in the images (Fig. 4), Ag2S nanoparticles are seen to form of larger, brighter spherical particles that are evenly distributed on the surface. In addition, EDX and Mapp analysis shows the type and dispersion of ZnS/SG QDS/Ag2S constituents. These two complementary analyses confirm the presence of oxygen, carbon, sulfur, zinc, and silver atoms in the photocatalyst. And it shows that the zinc element is the main constituent of the photocatalyst. The presence of oxygen can be being due to carboxyl groups of SG QDs and hydroxyl of adsorbed water on the sample (Fig. 4b,c).

To determine the size and morphology of ZnS/SG QDs/Ag2S photocatalyst, the transmission electron microscopy test was used. Fig. 5 shows that quantum dots of ZnS/SG have a spherical shape, core–shell structure, and a size of less than 5 nm. As you can see in the marked red box, the light grey shell of SG QDs surrounds the dark ZnS core. The ZnS/SG QDs display accumulations in some areas, which possibly due to the agglomeration of graphene quantum dots. This re-stacking of graphene plates is dependent on strong van der Waals forces and π-π interactions of sp2 structures. Furthermore, the Ag2S nanoparticles with different sizes and almost spherical shapes are clearly visible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of effective factors in optical degradation of the diazinon and fenitrothion
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidation rates and efficiency of the photocatalytic system are highly dependent on a number of operational parameters that govern the photodegradation of the organic molecule. The photodegradation depends on some basic parameters which are the amount of photocatalyst, pH of the solution, temperature of the reaction medium, time of irradiation of light, the intensity of light, dissolved oxygen in the reaction medium, nature of the photocatalyst, nature of the substrate, doping of metal ions and non metal and structure of photocatalyst. In the following the efficiency of these factors were evaluated in the designed photocatalytic system.

Increased turbidity due to high consumption of nanoparticles causes light refraction and reduced penetration of visible beam in the depth of the aqueous solution. As a result, the beam contact with nanoparticles and hydroxyl radical production are reduced. On the other hand, nanoparticles, tend to accumulate, especially in large amounts of catalyst, due to their high surface area and high surface energy. Therefore, it is necessary to optimize the dosage of the photocatalyst in order to prevent excessive consumption of the catalyst. For this purpose, different amounts of ZnS/SG QDs/Ag2S photocatalyst in the range of 1–25 mg for degradation of diazinon and fenitrothion pesticides were investigated and the 5 mg dosage of photocatalyst was selected as the best-consumed dosage (Fig. 6 ).

Irradiation time is one of the most important factors influencing Fenton oxidation processes. Photocatalytic degradation generally takes place in two stages; Step 1: Initial breakdown of the polluting molecule into less hazardous components. This step is checked by monitoring the UV spectrum. Step 2: breaking of all organic components until CO2 and H2O formation, which is one of the ways to measure the progress of this stage, the COD method and by calculating the amount of consumed oxygen. The results of the effect of irradiation time on the percentage of degradation are shown in Fig. 8
. As can be seen, the initial degradation of the two mentioned pesticides occurs in the period time of 35–40 min, but it takes much more time for the complete destruction of all organic components. Insofar as after 240 min, although the complete degradation of the diazinon molecule done, the degradation of the components of fenitrothion is almost 50%. The reason for the difference in the amount of COD between the two pesticides, despite the same catalyst and irradiation time, is probably related to photodegradation mechanisms (Fig. 7). It is guessed that the break of fenitrothion leads to the formation of the inert molecule, which is more resistant to photocatalytic oxidation.

Comparing UV and COD curves, it is apparent that initial organophosphorus pesticides degrade much faster than intermediate species. For this reason, the amount of COD is constant as long as it is present in the organophosphorus pesticide medium, but as soon as the initial organophosphorus contaminant is finished and the intermediates degradation begins, the COD also changes. Determining the exact mechanism of degradation according to LC-MS results is difficult due to similar intermediates and the very fast degradation of intermediates. There are three common step for the photodegradation of organophosphorus contaminants. Step I is on basis of hydrolysis of the phosphorus part and after that, degradation of the remained intermediates can continue the two pathways, i.e., demethylation pathway and dihydroxylation pathways which lead to decreasing in the molecular weight of new intermediates. In the three step, the opening of the aromatic ring leads to the small aliphatic acids and methanediamine (for the diazinon central ring) and finally water and carbon dioxide. The obtained central aromatic rings of diazinon and fenitrothion are pyrimidine and hydroquinone respectively. The pyrimidine ring contains two nitrogen heteroatoms that facilitate the photocatalytic degradation process compared to the stable molecule of hydroquinone. In addition, the toxicity of the photodegraded products was determined using the assay of acetylcholinesterase activity. The results showed that the photocatalytic reactions mixture after 60 min irradiation, had no effect on the activity of acetylcholinesterase.

The influence of temperature on the degradation of diazinon and fenitrothion pesticides in aqueous solutions and in the present of the ZnS/SG QDs/Ag2S photocatalyst was investigated in the range of 0–75 °C. The results are shown in Fig. 9 . The degradation efficiency gradually increases as the temperature increases. Based on this, it can be said that the process is exothermic and by drawing ln k (rate constant) versus the inverse of the temperature (kelvin) and in accordance with Arrhenius equation [86] (Eq. (6)), the activation energy of the reaction was obtained from the slope of the straight line. The value of the slope is equal to -Ea/R where R is a constant equal to 8.314 J/mol K (Fig. 10 ).
(6)
ln k = ln A -
E a
R T
The activation energy (Ea) of diazinon and fenitrothion is estimated to be 4.8 and 5.1 kJ/mol.

pH is one of the affecting parameters on the optical degradation yield that affects the decomposition of the target compounds, the distribution of the electric charge on the surface of the photocatalyst, and the oxidation potential of the capacitance band. The results of the pH effect in the Fig. 11 shown that the application of the photocatalytic process at pH = 3 has the highest percentage of degradation (about 98% for fenitrothion and 88% for diazinon after 30 min). The reason for the high percentage of degradation of pesticide at acidic pH (pH = 3) can be the more presence of H+ ions in the acidic condition, which leads to the formation of H+ radicals. In the following, these H+ radicals through the oxygen present in the solution form the HO-O• radicals which are capable of producing final hydroxyl radicals. High levels of hydroxyl radicals, leads to maximum destruction. Also, since the zero-point charge of zinc sulfide is at pH = 4.2 [87,88] and the pK of organophosphorus pesticides is acidic, the effect of pH on the photocatalytic process can be justified by the presence of electrostatic forces between the surface of zinc sulfide QDs and pesticide. Thus, at pHs above 4.2, the ZnS surface has a negative charge and the pesticide molecules also have a negative charge. Therefore, the force between them is repulsive and reluctant to reacts, resulting in the yield of photodegradation decreased at the alkaline pH. At pHs below 4.2, the ZnS surface has a positive charge and therefore the electrostatic force between the photocatalyst and the pesticide is the gravity force, which leads to an increase in degradation efficiency. Although, the highest percentage of pesticide degradation was obtained at an acidic pH but, due to the economic aspect and ease of operation treatment at the neutral pH, and also the possibility of dissolving metal catalysts at very acidic pHs. Investigation of other parameters in photocatalytic degradation of the mentioned pesticides was performed at pH = 7.

The synthesized photocatalyst in this project has three active components in advanced oxidation processes: ZnS QDs, SG QDs, and Ag2S. In order to investigate the effect of each component on the optical degradation efficiency, each of the mentioned components was used in the form of one-component and two-component in diazinon and fenitrothion degradation. The degradation percentages and kinetics rates are shown in Fig. 12 and Table 1 respectively.

According to the obtained data, Ag2S nanoparticles have the highest rate of degradation and catalytic activity, but due to the high cost and the possibility of accumulation of nanoparticles, their use as a single-component photocatalyst for environmental purposes is not cost-effective and reasonable. On the other hand, the ZnS QDs, despite their low cost, have a low catalytic degradation rate, destroying less than 30% of pesticides in 30 min, which is related to the high energy gap of these quantum dots. To solve this problem, and in order to increase the efficiency of ZnS QDs, SG coating was applied, which increased the degradation percentage by about 20%, but it is still not desirable. In the last step, small amounts of Ag2S nanoparticles were loaded on the surface of ZnS/SG QDs, which upgraded the photocatalytic properties and accelerated the degradation rate.

It should be noted that according to ICP analysis, the share of zinc and silver in the final catalyst is 26% and 0.9%, respectively which indicate the positive effect of all three photocatalyst components on the rate and percentage of degradation. Adding more than 1% of silver to ZnS/SG QDs causes the silver sulfide phase to form independently on the quantum dots surface, which reduces the photocatalytic properties due to the surface plasmonic effect and the zinc sulfide surface coverage.
Furthermore, the ZnS/SG QDs/Ag2S exhibit higher photocatalytic activity especially under LED light compared to other similar photocatalysts which are shown in Table 2 .

To have a functional photocatalyst, the stability and reusability of the catalyst is essential. In order to evaluate the recyclability of the ZnS/SG QDs/Ag2S, the synthesized photocatalyst was separated by centrifugation after each use cycle in the reaction and immediately washed with water and ethanol and dried at 60° C to be used for another cycle. This process was repeated for 4 consecutive cycles. As shown in the Fig. 13 , the efficiency of the photocatalyst after each cycle did not change significantly compared to the initial state, which indicates the stability of the ZnS/SG QDs/Ag2S photocatalyst.

In this study, the ZnS/SG core–shell quantum dots were synthesized and decorated with Ag2S nanoparticles. The synthesized photocatalyst was applied in visible-light degradation of diazinon and fenitrothion and shown high catalytic activity. The X-ray Diffraction investigation of the generated sample approved the successful synthesis of zinc sulfide nanocrystals with cubic structure. The average crystal size was estimated to be about 3.9 nm using the Williamson-Hall equation. Furthermore, the SEM and TEM images confirmed the core–shell structure of quantum dots in the photocatalyst. The results of the UV–Vis spectroscopy test also indicated that the quantum dots generated at room temperature have an energy gap of 3.9 electron volts, which is increased compared to the energy gap of the bulk ZnS. To reduce this energy gap, SG QDs and Ag2S were used, which had a positive effect on increasing photocatalytic properties. In the end, the recyclability and reusability of the catalyst were investigated, which maintained its properties during 4 cycles of use in the photocatalytic process.
